query: What is the Eightfold Path in Buddhism?
response: The Eightfold Path in Buddhism is a fundamental guide to ethical and mental development with the goal of freeing an individual from attachments and delusions. It consists of eight interconnected practices: ["right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19262), *Majjhima Nikaya 44 - Culavedalla Sutta*. This path is described as leading to the cessation of suffering and self-identification, offering a way to overcome passion, aversion, and delusion. As the Buddha explained, it is the path that leads to ["the cessation of kamma"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19446), *Samyutta Nikaya 35.145 - Kamma Sutta*.

query: How do different religions view the afterlife?
response:
Different religious traditions offer varied perspectives on the afterlife. In Islam, the Qur'án emphasizes that ["every soul shall taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_292) and that believers will be rewarded with eternal gardens, while those who disbelieve face a fiery recompense. Christianity similarly speaks of resurrection and judgment, with Jesus declaring, ["I am the resurrection, and the life"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_425), promising eternal life to those who believe in him. 

In Sikhism, the soul's journey is seen as a cycle of rebirths, and the Guru Granth Sahib notes that the soul ["wanders through countless incarnations"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20186), seeking peace. Buddhism, on the other hand, teaches that Nirvāṇa is the ultimate liberation from the cycle of rebirth, described as ["the highest happiness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_230), free from suffering. 

Hinduism offers a view where the soul is eternal, with Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita explaining that ["never the spirit was born; the spirit shall cease to be never"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bhagavad-gita?paraId=para_30), emphasizing the immortality of the soul. The Bahá'í Faith describes the afterlife as a continuation of the soul's journey, where it progresses in the presence of God, as Bahá'u'lláh writes, ["the soul will continue to progress until it attaineth the presence of God"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_1082). 

Taoism sees life and death as part of a natural cycle, suggesting that ["existence and non-existence, life and death were all ONE"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18768), reflecting a harmonious balance. Judaism, in texts like the Zohar, speaks of the spirit returning to God, as it is written, ["the spirit returns to Elohim who gave it"](https://oceanlibrary.com/zohar-volume-1?paraId=para_1161), highlighting a return to divine origin. Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to view the mystery of life beyond death.

Judaism echoes this sentiment with the command to ["love your neighbor as yourself"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18347), *Jerusalem Talmud Nedarim*, which is considered a great principle in the Torah.

In Islam, the Qur'án advises believers to return greetings with a better one, emphasizing reciprocity and kindness, as ["God taketh count of all things"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sura-iv-women_muhammad/?paraId=para_50), *Sura IV. Women*. The Bahá'í Faith extends this principle to a broader vision of unity, urging believers to ["live in peace, love, union and agreement"](https://siftersearch.com/document/11408), *Dwight Barstow Collection*, and to overlook the faults of others. Buddhism, while not explicitly stating the Golden Rule, embodies it through the teaching that ["mind is the forerunner of all actions"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-tu/?paraId=para_1501), *Sutra Collection (TU)*, suggesting that our intentions shape our interactions with others. Each tradition, in its way, underscores the profound impact of treating others with compassion and respect.

query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response: Buddhism and Hinduism offer distinct perspectives on the concept of self. In Buddhism, the teaching of anatta, or "not-self," is central. The Buddha explains that ["form is not self"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17268), as it is subject to change and dis-ease, *Samyutta Nikaya*. This view extends to feelings, perceptions, and consciousness, emphasizing that clinging to these aggregates leads to suffering. In contrast, Hinduism often embraces the idea of a true self or Atman. The phrase ["Thou art That"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19898), *The Tattva-Muktavali*, suggests an intrinsic connection between the individual soul and the ultimate reality, Brahman. While Buddhism denies a permanent self, Hinduism explores the unity of the self with the divine.

query: What do the Bahá'í Faith and Islam say about fasting?
response:
In the Bahá'í Faith, fasting is a spiritual practice observed from sunrise to sunset, as Bahá'u'lláh instructs believers to ["abstain from eating and drinking from sunrise until sunset"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16712), *The Most Holy Book (Kitáb-i-Aqdas)*. This period is seen as a time for spiritual awakening and increased mindfulness, as it helps to ["awaken and stimulate ideal advancements"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15203), *Star of the West*. In Islam, fasting during the month of Ramadan is a significant act of worship, where believers refrain from food and drink from dawn until sunset, as a means to purify the soul and develop self-discipline. The Qur'án emphasizes fasting as a way to achieve righteousness and spiritual growth.

Judaism also places importance on fasting, particularly on Yom Kippur, where it is commanded to ["afflict your souls"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17800), *Mishneh Torah, Rest on the Tenth of Tishrei*, as a form of repentance and reflection. In Hinduism, fasting is seen as a form of penance and self-control, with practices varying in intensity and duration, as described in the *Mahabharata* where fasting is linked to spiritual purity and devotion. Buddhism, on the other hand, incorporates fasting into the practice of Uposatha, where monks and laypeople observe a day of heightened spiritual practice, including abstaining from food at night, to emulate the arahants and cultivate mindfulness, as noted in the [*Anguttara Nikaya*](https://siftersearch.com/document/18943). Each tradition views fasting as a means to deepen one's spiritual connection and discipline.

query: What do the texts say about life after death?

Judaism offers a vision of the afterlife where the righteous enjoy the ["splendor of the Divine Presence"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18286), while in Buddhism, the Dhammapada teaches that those free from worldly desires attain ["Nirvāṇa"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_142). Hindu texts, like the Upanishads, speak of the soul's journey, emphasizing that the ["spirit dies not"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17366) but continues in a cycle of rebirth influenced by karma. query: Is there free will in religious thought?

Islam acknowledges divine guidance, stating that ["God leads astray whom He pleases and guides whom He pleases"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_palmer-tr?paraId=para_2407), highlighting divine sovereignty in human affairs. Hinduism, through the Bhagavad Gita, teaches that while actions are part of one's duty, true liberation comes from ["renouncing the fruit of action"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bhagavad-gita?paraId=para_276), emphasizing selfless action.

In the Bahá'í Faith, Bahá'u'lláh speaks to the nobility of human creation, urging individuals to ["rise then unto that for which thou wast created"](https://oceanlibrary.com/hidden-words-of-bahaullah?paraId=para_74), suggesting a purpose-driven existence. query: How do religions explain evil?
response:
Religions offer diverse explanations for the presence of evil in the world. In Sikhism, the concept of Maya is central — it is ["created by the One who has administered this potion"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20183), leading people into illusion and doubt, *Guru Granth Sahib - Anand Sahib*. The Bahá'í Faith, as explained by ‘Abdu’l-Bahá, views evil as ["the absence of good"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15205), akin to darkness being the absence of light, *Star of the West*. Hinduism addresses evil through the doctrine of Karma, suggesting that actions from past lives influence present circumstances, and that ["our acts are the results of free will"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19876), *The Great Indian Epics*. 

In Judaism, the Yetzer Hara, or evil inclination, is seen as a necessary challenge, with God promising to ultimately ["remove the heart of stone"](https://oceanlibrary.com/beginning-of-wisdom_rabbi-aryeh-lieb-lifkin), *The Beginning of Wisdom*. Islam recounts the story of Iblis, who refused to bow to Adam, becoming an ["enemy unto thee and unto thy wife"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16930), *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur'an*. Christianity, as interpreted by St. Augustine, describes a cosmic struggle between light and darkness, where some angels, driven by pride, became ["darkness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/city-of-god-and-christian-doctrine_augustine-of-hippo?paraId=para_1242), *City of God and Christian Doctrine*. 

Buddhism identifies ignorance as the root of unskillful qualities, leading to a cycle of suffering, where ["ignorance is the leader in the attainment of unskillful qualities"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-a-part-2?paraId=para_345), *Sutra Collection (A, Part-2)*. Finally, Taoism suggests that losing touch with one's true nature leads to the loss of virtue, as ["joy and anger lead astray from TAO"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18768), *Chuang Tzu - Mystic, Moralist, and Social Reformer*. Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to understand and address the concept of evil.

query: What is the nature of God?
response:
The nature of God is a profound and multifaceted concept across religious traditions. In Hinduism, Brahman is described as ["the Absolute, the One without a second"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17383), transcending all forms and manifestations, *The Upanishads*. The Taoist perspective holds that the ["Tao that can be told of is not the eternal Tao"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18799), emphasizing the ineffable nature of ultimate reality, *Tao Te Ching*. Bahá'u'lláh speaks of God as an ["unknowable, unattainable, and invisible"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16657) Absolute Reality, *Tablet of the Uncompounded Reality*.

Sikhism emphasizes the divine command or Hukam, with the Guru Granth Sahib stating that ["He Himself is the Creator; He Himself reveals the Hukam of His Command"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20183), *Anand Sahib*. Judaism affirms God's unity, declaring that ["the Lord he is God in heaven above and upon the earth beneath; there is none else"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18124), *Bereshit Rabbah*. 

Islam teaches the oneness of God, urging believers to worship with ["sincere devotion, being true in faith"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16933), *Sermon of Recognition with Luminousness*. Buddhism, while not theistic in the traditional sense, speaks of the Dharmakaya as the ["Ultimate Oneness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-l?paraId=para_293), transcending all dualities, *Sutra Collection (L)*. Each tradition offers a unique perspective, yet they converge on the idea of a transcendent, unifying principle that underlies all existence.

query: What is the relationship between faith and reason?
response:
The relationship between faith and reason is a nuanced one, explored across various religious traditions. In the Bahá'í Faith, 'Abdu'l-Bahá emphasizes that ["religion and science are the two wings upon which man's intelligence can soar"](https://oceanlibrary.com/paris-talks_abdul-baha), suggesting that both are necessary for true understanding. Similarly, the Hindu *Kena Upanishad* speaks of a reality that is beyond ordinary perception, urging seekers to recognize ["that which does not think by mind"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kena-upanishad?paraId=para_10) as the true essence of Brahman. In Christianity, Paul writes that ["the foolishness of God is wiser than men"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-first-epistlethe-corinthians?paraId=para_19), highlighting the limits of human wisdom compared to divine insight.

The Taoist perspective, as seen in the *Tao Te Ching*, suggests that true wisdom involves a kind of unknowing, where ["the Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18799), indicating that ultimate truth transcends verbal expression. Buddhism advises against relying solely on tradition or abstract reasoning, as the Buddha encourages a path that is ["immediately verifiable and capable of laying a firm foundation"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-hijk?paraId=para_1106) for moral discipline. In Judaism, the *Proverbs* declare that ["the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-book-of-proverbs?paraId=para_10), suggesting that reverence for God is foundational to true understanding.

Islamic teachings also caution against limiting God through human descriptions, as any attempt to do so is seen as disbelief in the divine essence. Sikhism, through the *Guru Granth Sahib*, emphasizes that within the mind are ["gems, jewels and rubies"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20185) if one listens to the Guru's teachings, indicating that true insight comes from spiritual guidance. Each tradition offers a unique perspective, yet all affirm that faith and reason are not mutually exclusive but rather complementary paths to deeper understanding.

This reflects the enduring nature of the covenant in Jewish thought, where it is seen as a lasting bond between God and the people.

query: What is progressive revelation, and which texts in the library discuss it?
response: Progressive revelation is the concept that divine guidance is revealed progressively over time through a series of messengers, each building upon the teachings of their predecessors. In the Bahá'í Faith, this is illustrated by the idea that ["the religion of God is the One Religion"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_719), with each prophet's teachings being a stage in the life of this singular faith, *Bahá’u’lláh and the New Era*. Similarly, the Qur'án acknowledges that ["We sent messengers before thee"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_4331), emphasizing a continuity of divine messages, *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur’án*. In Christianity, the concept is echoed in the promise of a ["new covenant"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-hebrews?paraId=para_109) that builds upon the old, *Hebrews*. The Sikh tradition recognizes this through the continuity of the Gurus, as seen in the Guru Granth Sahib, where the divine light is said to have passed through ["ten generations"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20196), *The Sisters of the Spinning Wheel - Sikh Poems*. Hinduism presents a series of divine descents or ["avatárs"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17369), each addressing the needs of the time, *The Ramayana of Valmiki*. In Judaism, the notion of a new covenant is prophesied in Jeremiah, where God promises to ["put my law in their inward parts"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-book-of-jeremiah?paraId=para_670), *Jeremiah*. Lastly, Taoism, while not focused on revelation in the same sense, speaks to the unfolding understanding of the Great Tâo, which is progressively apprehended through stages, as noted by Chuang Tzu in the *Texts of Taoism*.

query: True spirituality is really just about being a good person, isn't it?

David Bullen)*.

Islamic teachings emphasize sincere devotion and the importance of establishing prayer and charity as part of ["the true religion"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16933), *Sermon of Recognition with Luminousness*.
